feedback.text: I have a suggestion for this act. While the Digital Fairness Act addresses many important issues, I think it could also aim to stop video games from being "destroyed" by the companies and/or publishers that created them. Video games are bought to be played. That is their function and their intended value lies in the ability to play (and therefore use) them. It shouldn't be the case that a company can shut down their game in a way that makes consumers unable to play it after purchasing it. Usually there is not even a way for a consumer to get their money back if a game they own is made unplayable by the rights-owning company. One of the most famous cases of this happening was with a game called The Crew created partly by the video game company Ubisoft. Many companies might argue (and HAVE argued) that it's okay to "kill" video games because it's unreasonable to demand for games to stay playable forever. This, however, seems like a poor argument. There are countless video games that in fact are playable long after they've stopped receiving active support. It is not impossible to achieve without forcing companies to have obligations to maintain every part of their games forever. The European citizens initiative "Stop Destroying Video Games", which has surpassed 1,4 million signatures, contains all of the essential information about this issue and in my opinion proves the importance of it.
